macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Daf: Slaves as Subjects of Personal Status and Property Rights

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on tikun olam in Gittin and the comparative status of slaves and women
The dual dimensions of slave status: issura (personal status) and mamona (property ownership)

macro_segments.title: Mishna: Sale of a Slave to Non-Jews or Outside the Land of Israel

micro_segments.title: Mishna statement: if one sells his slave to non-Jews or outside Israel, he goes free
Rationale: slaves obligated in mitzvot; non-Jew will violate Shabbat and kashrus; exile loses mitzvot of Eretz Yisrael
Broader rabbinic concern: maintaining Jewish presence in Eretz Yisrael by preventing sale of property and slaves to non-Jews

macro_segments.title: Baraita Clarification: A Writ of Manumission Is Required

micro_segments.title: Baraita: although mishna sounds automatic, a get shichrur (writ of manumission) is actually required from the first master
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel: when no document is written, freeing is required; when a document is written, that itself constitutes his freedom
Rav Sheshet explains the form of the writ: a declaration that the master makes no claim upon the slave if he escapes, conditional on his freedom upon escape
Distinction between hefker (renunciation of ownership) and shichrur (change of status); the writ effects status change through ritual delivery

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Explanation: Non-Jew Can Only Own Labor, Not the Person; Master Retains Ownership to Effect Manumission

micro_segments.title: Tosafot resolves how a writ of manumission can take effect when the slave is outside the master's control: non-Jews can own only the labor (maasei yadayim), not the person
The master retains ownership of the slave's body (guf) as property and thus retains standing to free him even after sale to non-Jew

macro_segments.title: Baraita: Loan with Slave as Collateral—When Is the Slave Considered Sold?

micro_segments.title: Baraita: if you borrow money from a non-Jew with your slave as collateral (alav) and the non-Jew does his nimuso (applies his law/custom), the slave goes free
Rav Huna bar Yehuda: nimuso likely means a seal or mark the non-Jew places on the slave to show ownership
Rav Sheshet's objection: if nimuso means only a seal, why do we use the same term for fields taken as collateral, where no seal is placed?
Resolution: nimuso means the time (zman) for repayment has arrived; when the debt is due, possession shifts and obligation to free the slave is triggered

macro_segments.title: Gemara Resolution: Difference Between Slave and Field—Body vs. Immovable Property

micro_segments.title: Initial difficulty: in both slave and field cases, time for debt collection has arrived, yet different halachic outcomes
Tosafot's explanation: by the slave, the non-Jew has taken the body into his house (already in possession) even before the time fully arrives, forcing immediate mitzvot violations
By the field, the Jew only has the fruit (peira), not the body (guf); the field remains immovable and still owned by the non-Jew
Conclusion: once the slave is in the non-Jew's possession and forced to violate halacha daily, the obligation to free him is triggered

macro_segments.title: Baraita: Seizure for Debt vs. Forced Sale—Distinguishing Voluntary from Involuntary Acts

micro_segments.title: Baraita: if non-Jew collects the slave as payment of a debt (bechovo) without the master specifically putting him up as collateral, the slave does not go free
Baraita: if someone forces you to sell the slave (parhang / blackmailer scenario), the slave still goes free
Gemara: even in forced sale, you are held responsible because you had options to appease the person or offer alternative property
Contrast: if property is seized purely through non-Jewish military force without your choice or negligence, you are not held liable for the slave's freedom

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah's Questions: Temporary Sales, Conditional Labor, and the Status of Mixed Cases

micro_segments.title: Question: if you sell the slave for only thirty days with the understanding he returns afterward, must you free him when he returns?
Question: if you sell the slave on condition that the non-Jew does not work him, does that exempt you from the obligation to free him?
Question: if you sell the slave on condition he may observe Shabbat and Yom Tov, does that change the obligation?
Further questions: what if sold to a ger toshav (non-Jew who keeps the seven Noachide laws), a Kuti (Samaritan), or a Yisrael mumar (non-observant Jew)?
Gemara indicates that ger toshav is treated like a non-Jew; Kuti and Yisrael mumar remain debated; the principle concerns formal legal status vs. contextual reality

macro_segments.title: Practical Question: Can You Accept Monetary Compensation After a Forced Sale?

micro_segments.title: Scenario: non-Jew takes your slave without consent; you negotiate to accept money instead of freeing the slave
Rabbi Yirmiyah asks Rabbi Zerika to investigate whether this is permissible based on tannaic sources
Gemara: comparison to a house forcibly taken by non-Jew—when you cannot recover it, you are allowed to accept money and even register the sale in non-Jewish courts
Gemara's concern: people don't typically sell houses; if we allow money collection, we won't create a slippery slope. But slaves are more expendable
Rabbi Ami's resolution: based on baraita, if the slave is forcibly taken and cannot be recovered by any legal means, you may collect the money (just as with a house)

macro_segments.title: The Redemption Penalty: Forcing Purchase of the Slave at Elevated Prices

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi: if you sell your slave to a non-Jew, they can force you to buy him back at up to ten times (or one hundred times) his value
Gemara: is the number ten or one hundred precise, or approximate?
Baraita: selling an animal to a non-Jew requires redemption at ten times value; differing opinions on whether slave is also ten or one hundred times
Gemara: slave and animal penalties likely differ; animal might be ten times (more common), slave might be one hundred times (rarer transaction, harsher ongoing violation)

macro_segments.title: Liability of Heirs: Does the Obligation to Redeem the Slave Pass to the Children?

micro_segments.title: Question: if a master sells his slave and then dies, must his sons buy back and free the slave?
Consideration: on one hand, the fine targets the transgressor (issura); on the other, the slave suffers daily violations (ongoing issura)
Comparison to blemishing a firstborn animal (bechor): biblical violation might impose fine on heirs; here it's rabbinic
Comparison to forbidden work on Chol HaMoed: rabbinic violation; fine not imposed on heirs
Conclusion via baraita of field fertilized on Shmita: rabbinic fines are not imposed on heirs; the obligation ends with the transgressor
Additional case: non-visible damage (hezek she'eino nikkar) with rabbinic fine—fine not imposed on children

topical_tags.term: chutz la'aretz (outside the Land of Israel)
sheva mitzvos b'nei Noach (seven Noachide laws)
